channel.title: Studies Suggest
channel.description: Counterintuitive scientific findings, verified and well-sourced. Evidence that challenges what you think you know.

item.title: Taking Antioxidant Supplements Raises Your Risk of Dying. A Review of 296,707 People Exposed the Paradox.
item.description: A Cochrane review of 78 trials and 296,707 participants found that beta-carotene and vitamin E supplements increase all-cause mortality.

item.title: Open Offices Were Designed to Boost Collaboration. A Harvard Study Found They Cut It by 70%.
item.description: Two Fortune 500 companies outfitted employees with sociometric sensors before and after converting cubicles to open plans. Face-to-face conversation plummeted while email surged.

item.title: Repeating "I Am Lovable" Made People With Low Self-Esteem Feel Worse
item.description: Experiments at the University of Waterloo found that positive self-statements backfired for those who needed them most.

item.title: Brainstorming in Groups Produces Fewer Ideas Than Working Alone
item.description: A meta-analysis of 20 studies found that interactive brainstorming groups consistently underperformed individuals working independently.

item.title: Stretching Before Exercise Does Not Prevent Injuries
item.description: A systematic review of 25 RCTs found stretching had no significant injury prevention benefit, while strength training cut risk by over a third.

item.title: Exercise Burns Far Fewer Calories Than You Think. Your Body Adjusts the Books.
item.description: A study of 332 adults across five populations found total energy expenditure plateaus above moderate activity levels.

item.title: More Options Make People Less Likely to Choose — and Less Happy When They Do
item.description: Consumers were 10 times more likely to buy jam when offered 6 options instead of 24.

item.title: The Largest Registered Study of Video Game Violence Found No Link to Aggression
item.description: A pre-registered Oxford study of 1,004 adolescents using objective violence ratings found zero relationship with aggressive behavior.
